article_title: US Military Airlifts a Nuclear Microreactor for the First Time as Three Companies Race to Hit Criticality by July Fourth

content_preview.title: US Military Airlifts a Nuclear Microreactor for the First Time as Three Companies Race to Hit Criticality by July Fourth
content_preview.summary: Operation Windlord flew Valar Atomics' Ward250 reactor from California to Utah on three C-17s, kicking off an executive-order sprint to power military bases and data centers with factory-built nuclear plants.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

On February 15, the United States Air Force loaded a nuclear microreactor onto a C-17 Globemaster III and flew it nearly 700 miles from California to Utah -- the first time a nuclear reactor of any kind has been transported by military aircraft. The operation, designated "Operation Windlord," moved Valar Atomics' Ward250 reactor in eight modules across three C-17 flights from March Air Reserve Base to Hill Air Force Base, according to [The War Zone](https://www.twz.com/uncategorized...

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured News piece at 1,005 words, within the 400-1,200 word range for the category. The article uses four sections (Overview, What We Know, The Broader Microreactor Race, What We Don't Know, What Comes Next) to build a complete picture from operational fact through policy context to open questions. Writing is clear, precise, and technically accurate without being inaccessible.
editor_notes.source_verification: Seven sources cited, four on the allowlist (npr.org, interestingengineering.com, energy.gov, army.mil) and three off it (world-nuclear-news.org, twz.com, neutronbytes.com). All seven were manually verified. World Nuclear News is the publication arm of the World Nuclear Association and is the authoritative trade publication for this sector. The War Zone (twz.com) is an established defense journalism outlet with a record of breaking military-technology stories and is the primary source for operational details about this airlift. Neutron Bytes is a well-regarded individual nuclear-industry analyst blog run by Dan Yurman; it provides the critical perspective quotes from former DOE official Katy Huff and nuclear engineer Nick Turan. The NPR timeout during automated checks was a transient network issue; the NPR article was confirmed accessible and its content matches citations in the submission. All three off-allowlist domains are credible and appropriate for this specialist topic. Recommend adding world-nuclear-news.org, twz.com, and neutronbytes.com to config/source_allowlist.txt.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All verifiable claims match the cited sources. Key facts confirmed: February 15 airlift date; 62nd Airlift Wing conducting the mission; Operation Windlord designation; Ward250 technical specs (helium-cooled, graphite-moderated, TRISO fuel, 5 MW rated capacity); initial test phases (100 kW thermal scaling to 250 kW); reactor transported without fuel, with fuel to come from Nevada National Security Site; Chris Wright and Michael Duffey present at March ARB; July 4 criticality deadline from executive order signed May 2025; DOE's three supported developers (Westinghouse eVinci, Radiant Kaleidos, Antares R1) and their specifications; Army Janus Program with nine candidate installations; Katy Huff quote; $225,000 cost estimate for three C-17 flights; Edwin Lyman 'dog-and-pony show' characterization; Nick Turan radiation analysis; Valar commercial timeline (trial 2027, full 2028). No hallucinations or unsourced statistics detected.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional throughout. The article presents official statements alongside critical assessments without editorializing. Lyman's criticism and Huff's skepticism are given equal weight to the government officials' enthusiasm. No loaded language or sensationalism. The phrase 'Today is history' is correctly attributed as a direct quote rather than adopted as editorial framing.
editor_notes.originality: No existing articles on nuclear microreactors, Valar Atomics, Operation Windlord, or the Army Janus Program. Topic is entirely new to the publication.
recommendations: Add world-nuclear-news.org, twz.com, and neutronbytes.com to config/source_allowlist.txt — all three are credible specialist sources appropriate for defense and nuclear energy coverage.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: High-quality submission ready for publication. The article is factually accurate, well-sourced, neutrally framed, and covers a genuinely newsworthy event with appropriate breadth — from the operational milestone through the policy context to substantive critical perspectives. APPROVE.
